The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: MICROORGANISMS : FRIEND AND FOE MICROORGANISMS : FRIEND AND FOE, 2.1 Microorganisms, SCIENCE 18, MICROORGANISMS : FRIEND AND FOE 19. full_notes:
Microorganisms Friend and Foe is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that mICROORGANISMS : FRIEND AND FOE MICROORGANISMS : FRIEND AND FOE You have seen several kinds of plants and animals.

The text explains that however, there are other living organisms around us which we normally cannot see.

The text explains that for example, you might have observed that during the rainy season moist bread gets spoilt and its surface gets covered with greyish white patches.

The text explains that observe these patches through a magnifying glass.

The text explains that do you know what these structures are and where do these come from?

The text explains that 2.1 Microorganisms Activity 2.1 Collect some moist soil from the field in a beaker and add water to it.

The text explains that after the soil particles have settled down, observe a drop of water from the beaker under a microscope.

The text explains that spread on a glass slide and observe through a microscope.

The text explains that these observations show that water and soil are full of tiny organisms, though not all of them fall into the category of microbes.

The text explains that these microorganisms or microbes are so small in size that they cannot be seen with the unaided eye.

The text explains that some of these, such as the fungus that grows on bread, can be seen with a magnifying glass.

The text explains that others cannot be seen without the help of a microscope.

The text explains that that is why these are called microorganisms or microbes.

The text explains that microorganisms are classified into four major groups.

The text explains that these groups are bacteria, fungi, protozoa and some a l g a e.

The text explains that some of these common microorganisms are shown in Figs.

The text explains that this process of conversion of sugar into alcohol is known as fermentation.

The text explains that the source of these medicines is microorganisms.

The text explains that the nutrients released in the process can be used by the plants again.

The text explains that they can also get transmitted by direct contact with an infected person or carried by an animal.

The text explains that 2.5 Food Preservation In Chapter 1, we have learnt about the methods used to preserve and store food grains.

The text explains that low temperature inhibits the growth of microbes.

The text explains that this process was discovered by Louis Pasteur.

The text explains that mICROORGANISMS : FRIEND AND FOE 27 nitrogen cannot be taken directly by plants and animals.

The text explains that once nitrogen is converted into these usable compounds, it can be utilised by plants from the soil through their root system.

The text explains that (b) Blue green algae fix __________ directly from air and enhance fertility of soil.

The text explains that (a) Yeast is used in the production of (i) sugar (ii) alcohol (iii) hydrochloric acid (iv) oxygen (b) The following is an antibiotic (i) Sodium bicarbonate (ii) Streptomycin (iii) Alcohol (iv) Yeast (c) Carrier of malaria-causing protozoan is (i) female Anopheles mosquito (ii) cockroach (iii) housefly (iv) butterfly (d) The most common carrier of communicable diseases is (i) ant (ii) housefly (iii) dragonfly (iv) spider (e) The bread or idli dough rises because of (i) heat (ii) grinding (iii) growth of yeast cells (iv) kneading (f) The process of conversion of sugar into alcohol is called (i) nitrogen fixation (ii) moulding (iii) fermentation (iv) infection 3.

The text explains that you will find round structures called root nodules on the roots.

The text explains that bacteria have lived on the earth for much longer than human beings.

The text explains that now take another test tube filled 1/4 with lime water.

The text explains that remove the balloon from test tube B in such a manner that gas inside the balloon does not escape.

The text explains that fit the balloon on the test tube and shake well.

The text explains that mICROORGANISMS : FRIEND AND FOE 31 Did You Know?

The text explains that they are such hardy organisms that they can live under extreme conditions.

The text explains that they have been found living in boiling mudpots and extremly cold icy waters.

The text explains that they have been found in lakes of caustic soda and in pools of concentrated sulphuric acid.

The text explains that they can survive at depths of several kilometres.

The text explains that a kind of bacterium was recovered from a camera which stood on the moon for two years.There is probably no environment in which bacteria cannot survive.

Source: NCERT Class 8 Science, Chapter 2: Microorganisms Friend and Foe

revision_bullets: MICROORGANISMS : FRIEND AND FOE MICROORGANISMS : FRIEND AND FOE You have seen several kinds of plants and animals. (NCERT Class 8 Science Ch2 pg 1)
However, there are other living organisms around us which we normally cannot see. (NCERT Class 8 Science Ch2 pg 1)
For example, you might have observed that during the rainy season moist bread gets spoilt and its surface gets covered with greyish white patches. (NCERT Class 8 Science Ch2 pg 1)
Do you know what these structures are and where do these come from? (NCERT Class 8 Science Ch2 pg 1)
2.1 Microorganisms Activity 2.1 Collect some moist soil from the field in a beaker and add water to it. (NCERT Class 8 Science Ch2 pg 1)
After the soil particles have settled down, observe a drop of water from the beaker under a microscope. (NCERT Class 8 Science Ch2 pg 1)
Spread on a glass slide and observe through a microscope. (NCERT Class 8 Science Ch2 pg 1)
These observations show that water and soil are full of tiny organisms, though not all of them fall into the category of microbes. (NCERT Class 8 Science Ch2 pg 1)
These microorganisms or microbes are so small in size that they cannot be seen with the unaided eye. (NCERT Class 8 Science Ch2 pg 1)
Some of these, such as the fungus that grows on bread, can be seen with a magnifying glass. (NCERT Class 8 Science Ch2 pg 1)
